svg.title: IP vs Domain Reputation — What Controls What
svg.desc: Reference diagram showing that IP reputation and domain reputation are separate evaluations in email deliverability. IP reputation is tied to the sending server's IP address and is measured by protocols like PTR (RFC 1035) and SPF (RFC 7208), IP blocklists like Spamhaus, and Microsoft SNDS. Domain reputation is tied to the From: header domain and DKIM signing domain (RFC 6376), evaluated through DMARC (RFC 7489), Google Postmaster Tools Compliance Status, and Yahoo Sender Hub Insights. Some signals like spam complaints and bounce rate are attributed to both. Google and Yahoo offer domain-level reputation monitoring tools while Microsoft provides only IP-level monitoring via SNDS. When migrating ESPs, domain reputation travels with you but IP reputation resets. A common mistake is buying a dedicated IP to fix a domain reputation problem.

cc:Work.dc:title: IP vs Domain Reputation — What Controls What
cc:Work.dc:creator: InboxAlly
cc:Work.dc:subject: IP reputation, domain reputation, sender reputation, email deliverability, DKIM domain, SPF IP, PTR reverse DNS, Spamhaus, Google Postmaster Tools, Microsoft SNDS, shared IP, dedicated IP, ESP migration, domain warm-up, IP warm-up, DMARC alignment, From domain, signing domain
cc:Work.dc:description: Protocol-level comparison of IP and domain reputation showing which mechanisms are inherently IP-level vs domain-level, monitoring tools for each, portability differences, and decision guidance for ESP migration and dedicated IP decisions.

svg.text: WHAT CONTROLS WHAT — SEPARATE EVALUATIONS, DIFFERENT TOOLS, DIFFERENT PORTABILITY

g.aria-label: Core Distinction — IP Reputation vs Domain Reputation
g.data-description: IP reputation is tied to the sending server IP address and reflects the behavior of everyone who sends from that IP. Domain reputation is tied to the From header domain and DKIM signing domain and follows your brand regardless of which IP or ESP you send through.
g.text: Tied to the sending server's IP address
Reflects behavior of everyone who sends from that IP.
Shared IP = shared reputation. Dedicated IP = yours alone, starts from zero.
Tied to your From: domain + DKIM signing domain
Follows your brand regardless of which IP or ESP you send through.
Migrate ESPs → domain reputation travels with you. IP reputation does not.
Separate evaluations — you can have a clean IP with a damaged domain, or a strong domain on a problematic IP

g.aria-label: Protocol-Level Attribution — What Is Inherently IP-Level vs Domain-Level
g.data-description: Mechanisms that are inherently IP-level by protocol definition include PTR, SPF IP checks, IP blocklists, SNDS, and sending volume. Domain-level mechanisms include DKIM, DMARC, BIMI, Postmaster Tools Compliance Status, From header domain, and List-Unsubscribe. Spam complaints, bounce rate, and spam trap hits are attributed to both.
g.text: Maps IP to hostname, must forward-confirm
Record is in domain DNS, but evaluates the sending IP
Spamhaus SBL, CBL, XBL list IP addresses
Property of the sending server at that IP
Signature tied to d= domain in DKIM header
What the recipient sees, what providers associate
Attributed to the IP that delivered + the domain that sent
Hard bounces reflect on both IP and domain
Attribution varies by trap operator methodology

g.data-description: Ensure your ESP signs outbound email with your domain in the DKIM d= tag, not theirs. This ensures DMARC alignment across all providers and gives you ownership of your domain reputation wherever providers track it.
g.text: Make sure your ESP signs with your domain (d=yourbrand.com), not theirs.
Ensures DMARC alignment across all providers and gives you ownership of your domain reputation wherever providers track it.

g.aria-label: How to Monitor Each Reputation Type
g.data-description: Google Postmaster Tools provides both IP and domain reputation dashboards. Microsoft SNDS provides IP-level data only with no public domain reputation tool. Yahoo Sender Hub Insights provides domain-level data based on DKIM domains. Microsoft is the only major provider with no domain-level reputation monitoring tool.
g.text: SNDS — IP reputation, volume, trap hits

g.aria-label: Portability — What Happens When You Change Infrastructure
g.data-description: When changing ESP or sending provider, IP reputation resets to new IPs but domain reputation travels with you. When changing sending IP on the same ESP, IP reputation resets but domain reputation is unchanged. When changing From domain, IP reputation is unchanged but domain reputation resets.
g.text: WHAT HAPPENS WHEN YOU CHANGE INFRASTRUCTURE

g.aria-label: What This Means for Your Decisions
g.data-description: Three decision scenarios where the IP vs domain distinction changes the practical action: ESP migration, shared vs dedicated IP evaluation, and whether buying a dedicated IP will solve a deliverability problem
g.text: WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOUR DECISIONS
IP reputation does not (new ESP = new IPs).
If the problem is domain-level, migrating
ESPs won't fix it. If IP-level, it helps.
Check Postmaster Tools domain status first.
Shared IP: your domain reputation is yours
alone, but IP reputation is shared.
Microsoft (SNDS is IP-only): shared IP risk
Before buying a dedicated IP, check
If domain is the problem, fix list hygiene,
content, and engagement first. A new IP
starts at neutral — it won't fix a domain issue.

g.aria-label: Common Mistake — Buying a Dedicated IP to Fix a Domain Problem
g.data-description: The most common infrastructure mistake in email deliverability is buying a dedicated IP when the real problem is domain reputation. Domain signals including complaint history and engagement patterns follow the domain to the new IP.
g.text: Buying a dedicated IP to "fix" deliverability when the problem is domain reputation.
A new IP resets IP reputation to neutral but does nothing for a damaged domain. Complaint history and engagement patterns follow the domain.

g.data-description: Primary sources including IETF RFCs, Google Postmaster Tools documentation, Microsoft SNDS, Yahoo Sender Hub, and Spamhaus
